title: Antibiotic Stewardship
oneLiner: Systematic effort to optimise antibiotic use to improve outcomes, minimise resistance, and reduce adverse effects — every prescriber’s responsibility.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Sepsis from a resistant organism:** Patient on broad-spectrum antibiotics but no clinical improvement after 48h + history of prior antibiotics/healthcare contact → **think MDR pathogen** (e.g., ESBL, MRSA, Pseudomonas). NEW pattern: "failure to respond".
- **Clostridioides difficile colitis:** Any patient with new watery diarrhoea *while on* or *within 12 weeks* of antibiotics → **stop unnecessary antibiotics, isolate, send stool for C. diff toxin**. Warning sign: rising WCC, abdominal distension, hypotension.
- **Missed severe infection (e.g., meningococcal sepsis):** Non-blanching rash + fever + hypotension → **start IV antibiotics immediately** (ceftriaxone 2g IV). Do not delay for cultures if unwell — but do take blood cultures if <30 minutes.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (of inappropriate antibiotic use):** Lack of clinical improvement after 48-72h, new-onset diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/day), unexplained fever recurrence, rash or urticaria, acute kidney injury (e.g., oliguria with gentamicin).
- **Key exam findings:** Tachycardia, hypotension (sepsis from resistant bug), abdominal distension + tenderness (C. diff colitis), new maculopapular rash (drug reaction), jaundice (hepatotoxicity, e.g., co-amoxiclav).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Viral vs bacterial infection:** CRP <40, normal procalcitonin, no focal signs → likely viral; avoid antibiotics.
- **Asymptomatic bacteriuria vs UTI:** Pyuria on dipstick without dysuria, frequency, or fever → do not treat (except pregnancy, pre-urological surgery).
- **Colonisation vs infection:** Positive cultures from non-sterile sites (sputum, urine, wound swab) without systemic signs → do not treat.
- **Splitter:** Fever + rigors + focal signs → bacterial; no fever + clear sputum + typical viral symptoms → viral.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** NEWS2, lactate (VBG), urine dip, CRP point-of-care if available, chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC, neutrophils), CRP, procalcitonin (if available), U&Es (renal baseline), LFTs, blood cultures x2 sets (before antibiotics if possible, but do not delay in septic shock).
- **Imaging:** Chest X-ray (pneumonia), CT abdomen (suspected intra-abdominal source), ultrasound (abscess, biliary).
- **Special tests:** Stool culture + C. diff toxin (if diarrhoea), viral PCR (respiratory panel), procalcitonin trend (to rule out bacterial infection).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** qSOFA (≥2: RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered mental status) → high risk of sepsis; CURB-65 (pneumonia severity).
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures before first dose of antibiotics** — but if septic shock, give antibiotics first and take cultures within 30 min. Omission means you lose the chance to tailor therapy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Take cultures (blood, urine, sputum, CSF if indicated).
  - Start empiric antibiotics *only* if sepsis suspected (qSOFA ≥2, lactate >2, or clinical concern). Use local sepsis protocol (e.g., "Sepsis Six").
  - Record indication, name, dose, route, review date on drug chart.
  - Check allergies — if penicillin allergy, note reaction type (immediate vs delayed) and use alternative per local policy (e.g., doxycycline, clarithromycin).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Stop antibiotics at 48-72h** if no evidence of bacterial infection (negative cultures, falling CRP, afebrile, clinically improved). Document "antibiotics stopped – no infection."
  - **De-escalate** from broad to narrow based on sensitivities (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam → amoxicillin if sensitive E. coli).
  - **Switch from IV to PO** when improving (afebrile 48h, tolerating oral, no malabsorption). Use guidance: oral bioavailability >90% for most drugs.
  - **Set a stop date or review date** for every antibiotic prescription (e.g., "review in 72h").
  - Daily review: "Does this patient still need antibiotics? Any adverse effects? Can we narrow or stop?"
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete prescribed course — but do not extend unnecessarily. For uncomplicated infections (e.g., UTI, CAP) 5-7 days is often sufficient.
  - Document allergy status in hospital records.
  - Provide patient leaflet on antibiotic resistance and when to seek help.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids (Hartmann's or 0.9% saline, rate per NICE IV fluids guideline, reassess daily), VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin unless high bleeding risk), diet as tolerated, observations (NEWS2 q4h for sepsis, q1h if unstable). No NBM unless surgical cause.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (common scenarios — always check local formulary):**
  - Community-acquired pneumonia (low severity): amoxicillin 500mg PO TDS x5d (or doxycycline 200mg stat then 100mg PO OD x5d if penicillin allergic).
  - Urinary tract infection (uncomplicated): nitrofurantoin 100mg MR PO BD x3d (if eGFR >45).
  - Sepsis of unknown source: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV QDS (dose per local policy, e.g., 4.5g 8-hourly in some regions) — **do not invent; follow local sepsis protocol**.
- **Common alternatives:** co-amoxiclav, cefuroxime, metronidazole (for anaerobic cover), gentamicin (single daily dosing per local policy).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Penicillins + methotrexate → increased MTX toxicity.
  - Macrolides (clarithromycin, erythromycin) + warfarin → enhanced anticoagulation.
  - Quinolones (ciprofloxacin) + NSAIDs → increased CNS effects (seizure risk).
  - Tetracyclines (doxycycline) + antacids, iron, calcium → reduced absorption (separate by 2h).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gentamicin, vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, ciprofloxacin — **adjust dose or interval for eGFR**. Check local renal dosing guide.
  - Metronidazole, flucloxacillin — no adjustment needed in mild-moderate renal impairment but caution in severe.
  - Co-amoxiclav, cefuroxime — reduce interval if eGFR <30.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Gentamicin:** Check levels before second dose (trough <1 mg/L for once-daily, peak depending on indication). Failure to monitor → nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity.
  - **Vancomycin:** Trough levels should be 15-20 mg/L for serious infections; if not checked → AKI or subtherapeutic levels.
  - **Piperacillin-tazobactam:** High dose in renal impairment without adjustment → neurotoxicity (seizures, encephalopathy).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Clostridioides difficile colitis (diarrhoea, abdominal pain, toxic megacolon).
  - Drug-induced acute kidney injury (e.g., aminoglycosides, vancomycin, co-amoxiclav).
  - Severe allergic reactions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome, anaphylaxis).
  - Antibiotic-associated diarrhoea (non-C. diff).
- **Chronic:**
  - Emergence of multidrug-resistant organisms (MRSA, ESBL, carbapenemase-producing organisms).
  - Colonisation with resistant bacteria, increasing risk of future difficult-to-treat infections.
  - Loss of microbiome diversity (long-term health implications).
sections.Prognosis: - Good antibiotic stewardship reduces mortality from sepsis (timely appropriate therapy), decreases C. diff incidence, and slows resistance. Poor stewardship → 2-3x higher risk of resistant infections and adverse drug events. Most patients recover fully if therapy is appropriate and de-escalated.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **ALWAYS document the indication, name, dose, route, review date.** This single act reduces inappropriate continuation.
- **Use the "STOP" framework:** at 48-72h, ask "S — still necessary? T — toxicity? O — oral switch? P — plan for stop date?".
- **Check local prescribing policy before starting empiric therapy** — many trusts have antimicrobial guides integrated into the electronic prescribing system.
- **Review the drug chart daily** for antibiotics that can be stopped or narrowed – this is the single most effective stewardship intervention.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Treating positive cultures without clinical infection** (e.g., asymptomatic bacteriuria in elderly with catheter) → unnecessary antibiotics, C. diff risk.
- **Continuing antibiotics beyond the planned review date** without reassessing → resistance, adverse effects.
- **Not checking renal function before prescribing aminoglycosides or vancomycin** → AKI.
- **Using broad-spectrum antibiotics when narrow spectrum is indicated** (e.g., co-amoxiclav for cystitis when nitrofurantoin would work).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening fever, rigors, shortness of breath, new diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/day), rash or facial swelling, inability to tolerate oral antibiotics (vomiting).
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48h if cultures pending; if no improvement in 48h, contact hospital or attend ED. Complete full course as prescribed — do not stop early even if feeling better.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if rising).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (septic shock) — start fluids, call ICU, escalate antibiotics.
  - Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) not responding to 30 mL/kg crystalloid.
  - Suspected severe C. diff colitis (toxic megacolon, peritonism, WCC >35) — needs surgical review.
  - Anaphylaxis to an antibiotic — stop drug, call crash team, give IM adrenaline 0.5 mg.
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥7 or single parameter red — immediate review; GCS <13 (not due to sedation); lactate >2 with infection → escalate to sepsis team.

rawMarkdown:
## Antibiotic Stewardship
**One-liner:** Systematic effort to optimise antibiotic use to improve outcomes, minimise resistance, and reduce adverse effects — every prescriber’s responsibility.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Sepsis from a resistant organism:** Patient on broad-spectrum antibiotics but no clinical improvement after 48h + history of prior antibiotics/healthcare contact → **think MDR pathogen** (e.g., ESBL, MRSA, Pseudomonas). NEW pattern: "failure to respond".
- **Clostridioides difficile colitis:** Any patient with new watery diarrhoea *while on* or *within 12 weeks* of antibiotics → **stop unnecessary antibiotics, isolate, send stool for C. diff toxin**. Warning sign: rising WCC, abdominal distension, hypotension.
- **Missed severe infection (e.g., meningococcal sepsis):** Non-blanching rash + fever + hypotension → **start IV antibiotics immediately** (ceftriaxone 2g IV). Do not delay for cultures if unwell — but do take blood cultures if <30 minutes.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (of inappropriate antibiotic use):** Lack of clinical improvement after 48-72h, new-onset diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/day), unexplained fever recurrence, rash or urticaria, acute kidney injury (e.g., oliguria with gentamicin).
- **Key exam findings:** Tachycardia, hypotension (sepsis from resistant bug), abdominal distension + tenderness (C. diff colitis), new maculopapular rash (drug reaction), jaundice (hepatotoxicity, e.g., co-amoxiclav).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Viral vs bacterial infection:** CRP <40, normal procalcitonin, no focal signs → likely viral; avoid antibiotics.
- **Asymptomatic bacteriuria vs UTI:** Pyuria on dipstick without dysuria, frequency, or fever → do not treat (except pregnancy, pre-urological surgery).
- **Colonisation vs infection:** Positive cultures from non-sterile sites (sputum, urine, wound swab) without systemic signs → do not treat.
- **Splitter:** Fever + rigors + focal signs → bacterial; no fever + clear sputum + typical viral symptoms → viral.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** NEWS2, lactate (VBG), urine dip, CRP point-of-care if available, chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC, neutrophils), CRP, procalcitonin (if available), U&Es (renal baseline), LFTs, blood cultures x2 sets (before antibiotics if possible, but do not delay in septic shock).
- **Imaging:** Chest X-ray (pneumonia), CT abdomen (suspected intra-abdominal source), ultrasound (abscess, biliary).
- **Special tests:** Stool culture + C. diff toxin (if diarrhoea), viral PCR (respiratory panel), procalcitonin trend (to rule out bacterial infection).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** qSOFA (≥2: RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered mental status) → high risk of sepsis; CURB-65 (pneumonia severity).
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures before first dose of antibiotics** — but if septic shock, give antibiotics first and take cultures within 30 min. Omission means you lose the chance to tailor therapy.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Take cultures (blood, urine, sputum, CSF if indicated).
  - Start empiric antibiotics *only* if sepsis suspected (qSOFA ≥2, lactate >2, or clinical concern). Use local sepsis protocol (e.g., "Sepsis Six").
  - Record indication, name, dose, route, review date on drug chart.
  - Check allergies — if penicillin allergy, note reaction type (immediate vs delayed) and use alternative per local policy (e.g., doxycycline, clarithromycin).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Stop antibiotics at 48-72h** if no evidence of bacterial infection (negative cultures, falling CRP, afebrile, clinically improved). Document "antibiotics stopped – no infection."
  - **De-escalate** from broad to narrow based on sensitivities (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam → amoxicillin if sensitive E. coli).
  - **Switch from IV to PO** when improving (afebrile 48h, tolerating oral, no malabsorption). Use guidance: oral bioavailability >90% for most drugs.
  - **Set a stop date or review date** for every antibiotic prescription (e.g., "review in 72h").
  - Daily review: "Does this patient still need antibiotics? Any adverse effects? Can we narrow or stop?"
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete prescribed course — but do not extend unnecessarily. For uncomplicated infections (e.g., UTI, CAP) 5-7 days is often sufficient.
  - Document allergy status in hospital records.
  - Provide patient leaflet on antibiotic resistance and when to seek help.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids (Hartmann's or 0.9% saline, rate per NICE IV fluids guideline, reassess daily), VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin unless high bleeding risk), diet as tolerated, observations (NEWS2 q4h for sepsis, q1h if unstable). No NBM unless surgical cause.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (common scenarios — always check local formulary):**
  - Community-acquired pneumonia (low severity): amoxicillin 500mg PO TDS x5d (or doxycycline 200mg stat then 100mg PO OD x5d if penicillin allergic).
  - Urinary tract infection (uncomplicated): nitrofurantoin 100mg MR PO BD x3d (if eGFR >45).
  - Sepsis of unknown source: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV QDS (dose per local policy, e.g., 4.5g 8-hourly in some regions) — **do not invent; follow local sepsis protocol**.
- **Common alternatives:** co-amoxiclav, cefuroxime, metronidazole (for anaerobic cover), gentamicin (single daily dosing per local policy).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Penicillins + methotrexate → increased MTX toxicity.
  - Macrolides (clarithromycin, erythromycin) + warfarin → enhanced anticoagulation.
  - Quinolones (ciprofloxacin) + NSAIDs → increased CNS effects (seizure risk).
  - Tetracyclines (doxycycline) + antacids, iron, calcium → reduced absorption (separate by 2h).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gentamicin, vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, ciprofloxacin — **adjust dose or interval for eGFR**. Check local renal dosing guide.
  - Metronidazole, flucloxacillin — no adjustment needed in mild-moderate renal impairment but caution in severe.
  - Co-amoxiclav, cefuroxime — reduce interval if eGFR <30.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Gentamicin:** Check levels before second dose (trough <1 mg/L for once-daily, peak depending on indication). Failure to monitor → nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity.
  - **Vancomycin:** Trough levels should be 15-20 mg/L for serious infections; if not checked → AKI or subtherapeutic levels.
  - **Piperacillin-tazobactam:** High dose in renal impairment without adjustment → neurotoxicity (seizures, encephalopathy).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Clostridioides difficile colitis (diarrhoea, abdominal pain, toxic megacolon).
  - Drug-induced acute kidney injury (e.g., aminoglycosides, vancomycin, co-amoxiclav).
  - Severe allergic reactions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome, anaphylaxis).
  - Antibiotic-associated diarrhoea (non-C. diff).
- **Chronic:**
  - Emergence of multidrug-resistant organisms (MRSA, ESBL, carbapenemase-producing organisms).
  - Colonisation with resistant bacteria, increasing risk of future difficult-to-treat infections.
  - Loss of microbiome diversity (long-term health implications).

### Prognosis
- Good antibiotic stewardship reduces mortality from sepsis (timely appropriate therapy), decreases C. diff incidence, and slows resistance. Poor stewardship → 2-3x higher risk of resistant infections and adverse drug events. Most patients recover fully if therapy is appropriate and de-escalated.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **ALWAYS document the indication, name, dose, route, review date.** This single act reduces inappropriate continuation.
- **Use the "STOP" framework:** at 48-72h, ask "S — still necessary? T — toxicity? O — oral switch? P — plan for stop date?".
- **Check local prescribing policy before starting empiric therapy** — many trusts have antimicrobial guides integrated into the electronic prescribing system.
- **Review the drug chart daily** for antibiotics that can be stopped or narrowed – this is the single most effective stewardship intervention.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Treating positive cultures without clinical infection** (e.g., asymptomatic bacteriuria in elderly with catheter) → unnecessary antibiotics, C. diff risk.
- **Continuing antibiotics beyond the planned review date** without reassessing → resistance, adverse effects.
- **Not checking renal function before prescribing aminoglycosides or vancomycin** → AKI.
- **Using broad-spectrum antibiotics when narrow spectrum is indicated** (e.g., co-amoxiclav for cystitis when nitrofurantoin would work).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening fever, rigors, shortness of breath, new diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/day), rash or facial swelling, inability to tolerate oral antibiotics (vomiting).
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48h if cultures pending; if no improvement in 48h, contact hospital or attend ED. Complete full course as prescribed — do not stop early even if feeling better.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if rising).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (septic shock) — start fluids, call ICU, escalate antibiotics.
  - Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) not responding to 30 mL/kg crystalloid.
  - Suspected severe C. diff colitis (toxic megacolon, peritonism, WCC >35) — needs surgical review.
  - Anaphylaxis to an antibiotic — stop drug, call crash team, give IM adrenaline 0.5 mg.
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥7 or single parameter red — immediate review; GCS <13 (not due to sedation); lactate >2 with infection → escalate to sepsis team.
